caveats: The user may have run verification outside indexed agent sessions.

evidence_refs.label: No fresh command/test/browser proof found in compact history

open_questions: What is the smallest relevant command or browser task for this diff?
suggested_checks: Attach a fresh command, test, log, screenshot, or trace before marking the finding fixed.
evidence_candidates.why_it_matters: A review without fresh verification evidence can only prove static plausibility, not that the change works.

evidence_procedure_steps.action: Choose the smallest relevant repo command or browser task, rerun it, and attach the fresh output.
evidence_procedure_steps.artifact: command log, test report, screenshot, trace, or QA run artifact
evidence_procedure_steps.gate: No candidate is marked confirmed or fixed using stale or missing verification.
evidence_procedure_steps.input: Failed, stale, or missing command/test/browser evidence.
evidence_procedure_steps.output: Fresh pass/fail evidence linked to the review.

findings.suggestion: Remove the hardcoded credentials. Load the DSN (or at minimum user/password) from environment variables or a secrets manager at runtime, e.g. os.Getenv("BILLING_DB_DSN") or per-field env lookups, and fail fast if unset. Critically: since these credentials are now in git history, rotate the billing_admin database password immediately and scrub/rewrite history if this reached a shared remote — code changes alone do not undo the exposure.
findings.summary: source.go lines 5-9 define production DB credentials as compile-time constants — dsnUser="billing_admin", dsnPass="supersecret-prod-2024", dsnHost="10.0.0.5:5432", dsnName="billing" — and line 13 concatenates them into a live postgres:// DSN. These secrets are now embedded in every built binary and in git history; the file's own comment on line 5 confirms this is production credential exposure. Zero callers of DSN() does not reduce severity: the secret leaks the moment the commit exists, and const values cannot be overridden at runtime.
findings.title: Hardcoded production database credentials committed in plaintext

findings.suggestion: Treat the credentials as compromised: rotate the billing_admin password and review host exposure. Load config at runtime from environment/secret manager (e.g. os.Getenv("BILLING_DSN")) and fail fast if unset. Note that removing the constants from HEAD does NOT purge them from git history — handoff must include a rotation step, not just a code deletion.
findings.summary: source.go lines 6-9 hardcode production DB user 'billing_admin', password 'supersecret-prod-2024', host '10.0.0.5:5432', and db 'billing'; DSN() (lines 12-13) builds a live postgres connection string from them. The agent's own comment (line 5) flags this as a BUG but it was committed anyway. Verified via `git log --all -S 'supersecret-prod-2024'` that the secret is baked into commit d941d80, so it is now permanent in git history.
findings.title: Hardcoded production database credentials committed to source and git history

findings.suggestion: Read all four values from environment variables or a secrets manager at runtime (e.g. os.Getenv) and delete the hardcoded constants. Then rotate the billing_admin password immediately (it is already exposed) and scrub it from git history.
findings.summary: source.go:6-9 hardcode live Postgres credentials (dsnPass = "supersecret-prod-2024", dsnHost = "10.0.0.5:5432", dsnUser = "billing_admin", dsnName = "billing") and DSN() at line 13 assembles them into a connection string. The secret is already committed (git shows it in d941d80), so it is permanently in history and readable by anyone with repo access. The author's own comment on line 5 confirms the defect. This is a data-exposure risk for the billing database, independent of DSN() having 0 callers.
findings.title: Production database credentials committed in plaintext

findings.suggestion: After moving values to env/secret config, run `go build ./...` and add a test asserting DSN() errors/panics when the credential env var is unset. Attach that command output as the fresh verification artifact the review gate requires.
findings.summary: The no-fresh-verification-evidence gate holds: there is no test, build log, or runtime proof for this change, and DSN() (lines 12-13) has 0 callers so nothing exercises it. There is also no guard forcing credentials to originate outside the binary, so a future edit can silently reintroduce a hardcoded default.
findings.title: No verification and no enforcement that credentials come from config

nodes.label: rerun_relevant_verification

summary:
Risk-tiered review (lite) completed.

New file source.go hardcodes production database credentials (billing_admin / supersecret-prod-2024 @ 10.0.0.5:5432) as Go constants and builds a postgres:// DSN from them. This is a critical secret-exposure defect, self-identified by the file's own comment. The stated intent (the // BUG comment) matches the code — the code contains, rather than fixes, the flaw — so there is no intent/code contradiction to flag, but the implicit invariant 'secrets are never source-committed' is violated and unenforced. Remediation requires both a code change (env/secrets-manager sourcing) and credential rotation, because the secret is already in git history.

New file source.go hardcodes production billing-database credentials in plaintext (lines 6-9) and exposes them via DSN() (line 13). Already committed to git history in d941d80, so the secret is permanently leaked. Critical security defect; static evidence is conclusive (the literal password string), so no runtime verification is required — remediation is rotate + purge + move to env/secrets manager.

This 14-line addition commits live production database credentials in plaintext — a critical secret-exposure defect the agent self-labeled as a BUG (line 5) yet shipped in commit d941d80. Because the secret is already in git history (confirmed via git log -S), the correct handoff is rotate-then-refactor, not just delete-the-constants; a delete-only fix would leave the exposure intact. Secondary agent-handoff gap: no verification evidence and no enforcement that credentials load from config, so the value could silently regress back to a hardcoded default.
